title: Module 1 quiz — The OpenAI Mental Model

questions.prompt: Which mental model most accurately describes OpenAI's text models?

choices.text: A database lookup system.
A next-token predictor that has no memory between requests, a fixed context window, and pays per-token in and per-token out.
A persistent AI agent that remembers everything.
A search engine for the web.

questions.explanation: Every observable behavior of OpenAI's text models follows from: token-by-token next-token prediction, statelessness between requests, fixed context window, and per-token billing. Getting this mental model right is the foundation for every engineering decision in the course.

questions.prompt: You're starting a new OpenAI-powered product this week. Which API surface should you default to?

choices.text: Chat Completions — it has the longest track record.
Responses API — OpenAI's current primary surface. New features ship to it first, agent loops and tool use are cleaner, and reasoning model support is first-class.
Assistants API — managed agents are easier.
The legacy Completions endpoint.

questions.explanation: For new code, Responses is the default. Chat Completions is still fine and not deprecated, but Responses is where OpenAI is investing — cleaner state management, built-in tools, better reasoning support. Assistants is legacy with an official migration path; legacy Completions is deprecated.

questions.prompt: You need to categorize 50,000 customer support emails per day into 5 categories. Which model is the right starting point?

choices.text: Full gpt-5 for maximum quality.
A reasoning model — careful classification.
gpt-5-nano. Simple high-volume classification is exactly what nano is optimized for. At this volume, model choice is a 50× cost difference. Test quality; move up only if measurably needed.
An embeddings model.

questions.explanation: Nano models are optimized for high-volume simple tasks. Full GPT-5 would deliver effectively identical accuracy on this task at ~50× the cost. Always start with the smallest model that plausibly fits the task; move up based on data, not vibes.

questions.prompt: When does a reasoning model (gpt-5-thinking and similar) actually earn its higher cost and latency?

choices.text: On simple text generation and summarization.
On reasoning-heavy tasks: math, complex code, multi-step planning, careful logic. NOT for latency-sensitive UIs or simple formatting tasks.
On every task — it's strictly better.
Only on image generation.

questions.explanation: Reasoning models spend internal 'thinking' tokens before answering — measurably better on hard reasoning, but slower and 5–10× more expensive. They're worth the cost only when the task is genuinely reasoning-heavy and accuracy outweighs latency.

questions.prompt: Your chat product's API bill grew 40× from launch to 50 turns per average user conversation. What's the primary cause and the highest-leverage fix?

choices.text: OpenAI raised prices — wait for a discount.

Each turn re-sends the full conversation history. By turn 50, every call is paying for 50× the input tokens. Fix: implement conversation compaction (summarize old turns; keep recent turns verbatim) and prompt caching (stable content at the start of prompts). Combined: 70–90% cost reduction.
Hallucination — switch to a different model.
Bug in the SDK — file a ticket.

questions.explanation: Long conversations + stateless API + per-token billing = linear cost growth per turn. This is the most common cost trap in chat products. Compaction caps the input size at a stable level; prompt caching cuts cost on stable prefixes. Both are standard, neither hurts quality much.
